sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: All the S dots have been reassembled into magenta blocks. Now I can see 5 magenta blocks around the blob. Let me check their positions and figure out the correct strategy to complete the level without causing another game over.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['B', 'W', 'c', 'G']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']),
                  sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        if center[0] > 5:
            print(f"Node: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, center~({center[0]:.0f},{center[1]:.0f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I now have 4 magenta blocks at positions:
- (36, 18)
- (40, 36)
- (56, 46)
- (56, 16)
And the blob is at (27, 33) with 69 pixels.
Previously I had 4 blocks: (26, 28), (32, 34), (40, 38), (56, 16). After the game over, the blocks scattered and I reassembled them, but now I only have 4 blocks instead of the original 4 (plus the one at (26, 28) which was already adjacent).
Wait, looking at the original level, I had 4 magenta blocks. After the game over, they scattered into S dots. I clicked on the S dots to reassemble them, and now I have 4 magenta blocks again. But the positions are different from the original.
The key insight is: when I moved blocks too close to the blob (clicking at a position adjacent to the blob), it caused a game over. So I need to be more careful about where I click.
Looking at the header: S, M, p, Y. The progression seems to be:
- S (sky blue) = scattered/dead state
- M (magenta) = active block
- p (purple) = placed/absorbed block
- Y (yellow) = target/complete
The yellow block at (4, 32) with 16 pixels might be the target or a reference.
Now I need to figure out the correct way to place the blocks. Previously, when blocks were adjacent to the blob, they changed from M to p. But when I clicked too close, the game ended.
Let me think about the level design. The blob is at (27, 33). The blocks need to be placed at specific positions around the blob. Looking at the original positions:
- (26, 28) - left of blob
- (32, 34) - below blob
- (40, 38) - below-right of blob
- (56, 16) - far below-left
The blocks at (26, 28) and (32, 34) were already adjacent to the blob and had changed to purple. The blocks at (40, 38) and (56, 16) needed to be moved closer.
But when I moved (40, 38) to (36, 38), it became purple. Then when I tried to move (56, 16) closer and clicked at (40, 30), the game ended.
So the mechanic seems to be:
1. Move blocks toward the blob
2. When they get adjacent, they change to purple
3. But if you click too close (maybe directly on the blob or in a forbidden zone), the game ends
The issue is I don't know the exact target positions for each block. Let me try a different approach: move each block one step at a time toward the blob, and stop when they become adjacent (change to purple).
Current blocks:
- (36, 18) - need to move toward (27, 33)
- (40, 36) - need to move toward (27, 33)
- (56, 46) - need to move toward (27, 33)
- (56, 16) - need to move toward (27, 33)
Let me move them one at a time, checking after each move if the block changed color.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move block at (36, 18) toward blob at (27, 33)
# Move up and right: click (32, 22)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 22}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
